coredata.dc:title: Fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with stable and strong electrochemiluminescence as an emitter for ultrasensitive detection of HIV-DNA fragment
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jie
Zhang, Yue
Yuan, Ruo
Chai, Yaqin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) with stable and strong electrochemiluminescence (ECL) was used as a novel ECL emitter for constructing an ultrasensitive biosensor to detect human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) DNA fragment. The doping of fluorine and nitrogen in the carbon dots (CDs) not only effectively formed hydrogen bonds to hinder the free rotation of CDs for increasing the radiation transition probability, but also changed the charge distribution of CDs leading to a narrower energy gap, which improved the ECL intensity and stability of FNCDs. Moreover, exonuclease III (Exo III) -assisted multiple recycling amplification could convert the trace HIV-DNA fragment into a large amount of output DNA to trigger horizontal hybridization chain reaction (H-HCR) to form Y-supported three-dimensional (3D) network DNA nanostructure for immobilizing a lot of doxorubicin-ferrocenecarboxylic acid (Dox-FcCOOH) as ECL quencher, which significantly improved the sensitivity of the proposed biosensor. Thus, the fabricated ECL biosensor realized ultrasensitive detection of HIV-DNA fragment in the concentration range of 10 aM to 10 pM with a detection limit as low as 6.34 aM. This strategy provided a novel ECL emitter to construct biosensors for detection of biomolecule and early clinical disease diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence biosensor
Fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
Signal amplification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the proposed ECL biosensor
Stability and selectivity of ECL biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation (NNSF) of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (NNSF) of China ( 21974108 , 22176153 and 22174113 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of FNCDs (inset: lattice spacing). (B) the corresponding particle size distribution image. (C) The AFM image of FNCDs. (D) the corresponding height profile of the FNCDs.
(A) XPS survey spectra of FNCDs. (B-D) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and F 1 s (F) The DSC curves of the FNCDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of FNCDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of FNCDs. (C) FL spectra of FNCDs (inset: excitation (yellow line) and emission (white line)) spectrum. (D) ECL spectrum of FNCDs.

(A) ECL responses during biosensor fabrication process: Ag NPs/FNCDs/GCE (a), HT/Y-shaped DNA /Ag NPs/FNCDs/GCE (b), Dox-FcCOOH/output DNA/HT/Y-shaped DNA /Ag NPs/FNCDs/GCE (c). (B) CV characterization performed in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−solution (5 mM): bare GCE (a), Ag NPs/FNCDs/GCE (b), Y-shaped DNA /Ag Nps/FNCDs/GCE (c), HT/Y-shaped DNA /Ag NPs/FNCDs/GCE (d), output DNA /HT/Y-shaped DNA /Ag NPs/FNCDs/GCE (e). (C) ECL intensity of the biosensor toward HIV-DNA fragment with different concentrations range of 10 aM to10 pM. (D) Corresponding calibration plot of HIV-DNA fragment detection.
(A) Stability of the ECL biosensor incubated with 1 pM and 10 fM HIV-DNA fragment under 15 repetitive cyclic potential scans. (B) Selectivity of the biosensor towards HIV-DNA fragment.

Principles of constructing biosensors: (A) preparation of the FNCDs, (B) constructiing ECL biosensor, (C) preparation process of the Y-shaped DNA and (D) exonuclease III-assisted multiple recycling amplification process.
Comparison of this biosensor with other works for detection of HIV-DNA fragment.

head.ce:title: Fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with stable and strong electrochemiluminescence as an emitter for ultrasensitive detection of HIV-DNA fragment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) with stable and strong electrochemiluminescence (ECL) was used as a novel ECL emitter for constructing an ultrasensitive biosensor to detect human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) DNA fragment. The doping of fluorine and nitrogen in the carbon dots (CDs) not only effectively formed hydrogen bonds to hinder the free rotation of CDs for increasing the radiation transition probability, but also changed the charge distribution of CDs leading to a narrower energy gap, which improved the ECL intensity and stability of FNCDs. Moreover, exonuclease III (Exo III) -assisted multiple recycling amplification could convert the trace HIV-DNA fragment into a large amount of output DNA to trigger horizontal hybridization chain reaction (H-HCR) to form Y-supported three-dimensional (3D) network DNA nanostructure for immobilizing a lot of doxorubicin-ferrocenecarboxylic acid (Dox-FcCOOH) as ECL quencher, which significantly improved the sensitivity of the proposed biosensor. Thus, the fabricated ECL biosensor realized ultrasensitive detection of HIV-DNA fragment in the concentration range of 10 aM to 10 pM with a detection limit as low as 6.34 aM. This strategy provided a novel ECL emitter to construct biosensors for detection of biomolecule and early clinical disease diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) technology was a powerful analytical method to detect biomarkers owing to its advantages of high sensitivity, low background signal and wide dynamic [1–4]. In recent years, there has been an increasing interest in the employment of carbon dots (CDs) in ECL biosensing systems for the detection of analytes related to clinical diagnostics, such as nucleic acids, proteins and so on [5–7]. It is noteworthy that N-doping has found to be an effective approach to optimize the surface state for improving the ECL performance of CDs [8–10]. However, the ECL efficiency of single atom doping CDs was still unsatisfactory due to the restricted active sites, which limited its application in the ECL field. Recently, multiatoms co-doping in CDs with unique electronic structure, such as N/S co-doping, N/P co-doping and N/B co-doping, was used to improve ECL efficiency [11–14]. However, the ECL efficiency of multiatoms co-doped CDs was still insufficient due to the free rotation of CDs, which increased the non-radiative transition, thus the synthesis of multiatoms co-doped CDs with high ECL efficiency was still challenging. In this work, we successfully synthesized a highly electronegative fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CDs (FNCDs) as a novel ECL emitter, which hindered the free rotation of FNCDs by forming hydrogen bonds to decrease non-radiative transition, resulting in high ECL response and amazing ECL stability.

Herein, a novel ECL biosensor was constructed with FNCDs as an efficient ECL emitter and ingenious horizontal hybridization chain reaction (H-HCR) coupled with exonuclease III (Exo III)-assisted multiple recycling amplification as nucleic acid amplification strategy to detect human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) DNA fragment. The FNCDs was synthesized by solvothermal method using glucose and (C2H5)3N·3HF as raw materials ( Scheme 1A). For the electrode modification in Scheme 1B, the FNCDs and Ag nanoparticles (NPs) were electrostatically adsorbed on the electrode surface successively, and afterwards Y-shaped DNA formed by three single strands DNA (Ya, Yb and Yc) and two hairpins DNA (H2 and H3) (Scheme 1C) was fixed on the electrode via silver sulfhydryl bonds. In the presence of the output DNA converted by target DNA in the aid of Exo III-assisted multiple recycling amplification (Scheme 1D), hairpin H2 was opened, and then the expanded H2 triggered a cascade reaction to open hairpin H3, so that the H-HCR occurred between the H2 and H3 to form Y-supported three-dimensional (3D) network DNA nanostructure. Finally, the massive synthetic compound doxorubicin-ferrocenecarboxylic acid (Dox-FcCOOH) was inserted into the Y-supported 3D network DNA nanostructure through the Dox with excellent duplex insert-ability to distinctly quench the ECL signal of FNCDs. Significantly, H-HCR overcame the disadvantage of unsatisfactory sensitivity and reaction kinetics caused by conventional longitudinal linear HCR, which significantly increased the proposed biosensor's sensitivity due to the shortening of the distance between electron and electrode surface. Therefore, the proposed biosensor realized the ultrasensitive detection of HIV-DNA fragment reaching a detection limit of 6.34 aM and provided an effective material for the detection of biomolecules and early diagnosis of diseases.

The FNCDs was prepared via a one-step solvothermal process [15]. Firstly, we placed 1 g of glucose and 2 mL of triethylamine trihydrofluoride (Et3N•3HF) in a Teflon lined autoclave containing 40 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF)/acetonitrile (ACN) (1:1) mixture, and then reacted at 200 ℃ for 12 h. Secondly, vacuum rotary evaporation was used to remove part of the solvent at 80 ℃. Thirdly, the black solution was washed with ethanol and centrifuged to collect precipitation. Finally, the precipitate was dried to obtain FNCDs for further use.

The preparation of the Y-shaped scaffold was performed by mixing three single-stranded DNA (Ya, Yb and Yc) at equal concentration (5 μM) and volume (100 μL), and then hybridizing them at 37 ℃ for 2 h. Subsequently, the 100 μL of H2 (5 μM) and 100 μL of H3 (5 μM) were mixed with the Y-shaped scaffold and the reaction was carried out at 37 ℃ for 2 h to assemble the Y-shaped DNA.

Firstly, the H1(2 μM) was slowly annealed to form a hairpin structure. Secondly, equal volumes (100 μL) of H1(2 μM) and P (2 μM) were blended to form H1-P hybrid under reaction conditions of 30 min at 25 ℃. Afterwards, 100 μL of HIV-DNA fragment containing Exo III (6 U/μL) at different concentrations was added to H1-P hybrid and reacted together at 37 ℃ for 30 min. Finally, Exo III was inactivated by placing the solution at 70 °C during 10 min and retained the prepared output DNA at − 4 °C.

For electrode modification, 10 μL of FNCDs (1 mg/mL) aqueous solution was placed in electrode and kept at 37 ℃ for 3 h. Then 10 μL of Ag NPs (Supplementary Information, page S5) was dropped and dried at 37 ℃ for 2 h to gain Ag NPs/FNCDs/GCE. Next, 10 μL of Y-shaped DNA was incubated on Ag NPs/FNCDs/GCE overnight at 4 ℃ to ensure their immobilization on the electrode surface by Ag-S bonds. Afterwards, the electrode was blocked at room temperature during 30 min by 10 μL of hexanethiol (1 mM) for removal of nonspecific adsorption. Thereafter, the H-HCR reaction was triggered by 10 μL of output DNA at various concentrations and the reaction conditions was 37 ℃ for 2.5 h. Next, 10 μL DOX-FcCOOH (Supplementary Information, page S6) was dropped on surface to quench the ECL response. Finally, the ECL signal was measured by an MPI-A ECL analyzer set to a potential sweep of 0 to − 2 V and a photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage of 800 V in 2 mL of PBS containing 15 mM S2O8 2−.

The morphology of the prepared FNCDs was characterized. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of FNCDs ( Fig. 1 A) showed that FNCDs had a spherical particle shape averaging approximately 2.5 nm in diameter (Fig. 1B). In the inset of Fig. 1 A, the lattice parameter of individual FNCDs was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of a graphite. Moreover, the atomic force microscope (AFM) image (Fig. 1 C) showed the spherical configuration of the FNCDs with about 1.6 nm height (Fig. 1D), indicating their monolayered structure.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) demonstrated the chemical compositions and surface functional groups of FNCDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, FNCDs contained C, N, O and F elements, with surface atomic concentration of 16.3 % and 5.1 % for nitrogen and fluorine, respectively (Table S2). The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 2B) displayed five fitting peaks at 284.1 eV (CC), 284.8 eV (C-N/C-C), 285.9 eV (C-O), 286.6 eV (CN/C-O) and 288.4 eV (C-F). The high-resolution spectra of N 1 s (Fig. 2 C) demonstrated three fitting peaks: CN bonds (398.6 eV), amino-N (399.8 eV) and N-oxides (402.6 eV). The high-resolution O 1 s (Fig. 2D) spectrum exhibited three fitting peaks: CO (532.0 eV), C-O (532.5 eV), and COOR (533.8 eV). The high-resolution of F 1 s (Fig. 2E) spectrum had three fitting peaks: semi-ionic C-F (685.9 eV), covalent C-F (686.6 eV) and -CF2 (687.8 eV) [15,16].

The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and variable temperature FTIR was used to characterize the hydrogen bonds interaction of the samples. As shown in Fig. 2 F, DSC curves of FNCDs display two broad endothermic peak at 46 °C and 125 °C on heating from 0 °C to 160 °C, which showed that there exist hydrogen bonds. The broad endotherm at 10–80 °C could be owed to the scission of intradot hydrogen bonds, and the subsequent endothermic peak could be owed to the scission of interdot hydrogen bonds. In addition, variable temperature fourier transform infrared (FTIR) was a widespread and effective method for studying hydrogen bonding, which shows that an increase in temperature will result in a decrease in the average strength of the hydrogen bond resulting in a decrease in absorbance intensity [17–20]. As shown in Fig S8, the peak at about 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the hydrogen-bonded N-H stretching vibration and the absorption peak of the stretching vibration of C-F appeared at 1259 cm−1, where the N-H groups were hydrogen bonded with C-F. As the temperature increases from 20 °C to 150 °C, the absorption peaks of hydrogen-bonded N-H and C-F gradually decreased, indicating a gradual breaking of the hydrogen bond during heating.

To further analyze the optical properties of the FNCDs, a series of spectroscopic characterizations were implemented. Fig. 3 A showed the UV–vis absorption spectra of FNCDs with two main absorption peaks at 283 nm and 314 nm, possibly owing to the π → π * transition of the sp2 domain and n → π * transition, respectively [21,22]. Furthermore, Fig. 3B displayed the FTIR spectra of FNCDs with vibrational absorption peak at N-H bond (3440 cm−1), CO bond (1633 cm−1), C-N bond (1392 cm−1) and C-F bond (1118 cm−1 and 1257 cm−1) [15,23,24]. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3 C) displayed that FNCDs had a maximum emission located at 388 nm with excitation at 237 nm. Interestingly, the ECL spectra of FNCDs (Fig. 3D) showed the largest ECL emission peak located at 537 nm, exhibiting a significant redshift compared to the fluorescence spectra, due to the different emission mechanisms between ECL and fluorescence [25–27].

The possible ECL emission process of FNCDs was described as follows. During the scanning of the electrode potential in the cathodic direction, FNCDs and S2O8 2- generated a large number of FNCDs•- and SO4 •- on the surface of GCE, respectively. Then, SO4 •- could reacted with FNCDs•- to produce abundant FNCDs* , and eventually FNCDs* emitted light on return to the ground state, resulting in a high ECL efficiency of FNCD up to 14.18 % (Supplementary Materials, page S11).
(1) FNCDs + e- →FNCDs•-
(2) S2O8 2- + e- →SO4 2- + SO4 •-
(3) FNCDs•- + SO4 •- →FNCDs* + SO4 2-
(4) FNCDs*→FNCDs + hv

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the proposed ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS) caused by HIV was an infectious disease, which could be diagnosed early and rapidly by nucleic acid detection of HIV-DNA fragment in host cells immediately after infection regardless of the window period [28–31]. In addition, HIV-DNA load was clinically relevant and could predict the progression of AIDS and evaluate the effectiveness of treatment [32]. The ECL and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were applied to verify the successful construction of the proposed biosensor. In Fig. 4 A, a strong ECL signal appeared after Ag NPs and FNCDs were added on the GCE (signal-on, curve a). Subsequently, the ECL signal decreased slightly when the Y-shaped DNA and hexanethiol (HT) were incubated successively on the modified electrode (curve b). Finally, when H-HCR was triggered by output DNA to form Y-supported 3D network DNA nanostructure for immobilizing quencher Dox-FcCOOH, the ECL signal was greatly reduced (signal-off, curve c). The CV curves were recorded at each step (Fig. 4B), and the redox peak current decreased significantly after incubation of FNCDs and Ag NPs because FNCDs suppressed the electrons transfer, suggesting that FNCDs and Ag NPs were successfully attached to the GCE successively (curve b). When the negatively charged Y-shaped DNA was added, the electrostatic repulsion between Y-shaped DNA and [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– lead to a gradual decrease of the redox peak currents (curve c). Subsequently, blocking of nonspecific sites with HT lead to a slight restoration of redox peak current, due to a reduction of negative charge and a change in Y-shaped DNA conformation, which was more ordered (curve d) [33]. Afterwards, the output DNA triggered H-HCR reaction to form a Y-supported 3D network DNA nanostructure, which greatly hindered electrons transfer and reduced the redox current (curve e). Similarly, the characterization of 16 % polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) showed that the interactions between these DNA sequences occurred as expected, which reaffirmed the feasibility of the scheme (Fig S1).

The ECL response to HIV-DNA fragment were tested under optimal experimental conditions (Fig S2). As illustrated in Fig. 4 C, the logarithm of HIV-DNA fragment concentration showed a liner relationship with the ECL signal, and as the HIV-DNA fragment increased, the detected ECL signal decreased gradually. In Fig. 4D, the linear regression equation was I = 10366.18 – 2000.57 lg c (R =0.998). Similar results were obtained when the ECL responses were normalized to the highest value of 0-does analyte (Fig S3). At the same time, the limit of detection (LOD) derived from LOD = 3S
B
/ m was calculated as low as 6.34 aM, the calculation of which was described in the Supporting Information, page S9. Compared with the previous reports on detection of HIV-DNA fragment, the biosensor showed better sensitivity, suggesting that this strategy had a great promise for application in bioassays ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and selectivity of ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL responses with various concentration of HIV-DNA fragment (1 fM and 1 pM) was assessed through continuous cyclic potential scanning. In Fig. 5 A, the ECL responses did not change significantly after 15 cycles of cyclic scanning, suggesting a desirable stability of this biosensor. Furthermore, the ECL responses of three interferences (miRNA-21, hepatitis B virus (HBV) DNA and MTB-DNA) were studied to assess the selectivity of the proposed biosensor. There was no significant difference between the ECL responses of the interferences and the blank group (Fig. 5B), whereas the ECL signal was markedly weakened in the existence of HIV-DNA fragment (1 pM). In addition, a mixture solution of the three interferences mentioned above (1 pM HIV-DNA fragment containing 10 pM miRNA-21, 10 pM hepatitis B virus (HBV) DNA and10 pM mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB) DNA) showed an ECL response approximating the ECL intensity of 1 pM HIV-DNA fragment. As a result, the prepared biosensor had excellent selectivity. Moreover, the low cytotoxicity of the FNCDs (Fig S5) indicated the good biocompatibility of the biosensor.

Recovery experiments were carried out with diluted 10-fold human serum containing different concentrations of HIV-DNA fragment (100, 1000, 10,000, and 100,000 aM) under the same test conditions to assess the effectiveness of fabricated sensor, and the results showed good recoveries ranged from 93.5 % to 105.0 % with relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 5% (Table S3), indicating the proposed biosensor had good potential for application in practical samples.

In conclusion, a highly sensitive ECL biosensor for HIV-DNA fragment detection was constructed based on FNCDs as ECL emitter and H-HCR coupled with Exo III-assisted multiple recycling amplification as a nucleic acid amplification strategy. Firstly, FNCDs as a novel ECL emitter showed the advantages of excellent ECL performance and good biocompatibility with S2O8 2- as the coreactant, which could be attributed to the fact that fluorine and nitrogen not only formed a large number of strong hydrogen bonds to hinder the FNCDs free rotation and reduce the non-radiative relaxation of excited states, but also changed the charge distribution on the surface to obtain a smaller surface state energy gap. Secondly, the Y-supported 3D network DNA nanostructure amplified by H-HCR were different from the simple linear amplification of conventional HCR, which shortened the electron transfer distance between ferrocene and electrode, making the detection of HIV-DNA fragment more sensitive. Thirdly, Exo III-assisted multiple recycling amplification could convert trace HIV-DNA into an abundance of output DNA to achieve the amplification of the target nucleic acid to trigger H-HCR for immobilizing large amounts of Dox-FcCOOH. Therefore, combining these advantages, this strategy has a broad application prospect in biochemical analysis and early clinical diagnosis.

Jie Liu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yue Zhang: Data curation, Investigation. Ruo Yuan: Modified, Funding acquisition. Yaqin Chai: Modified, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (NNSF) of China (21974108, 22176153 and 22174113).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.133260.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.Y. Fang, C. Li, J. An, S.D. Zhao, Z.Y. Zhuang, Y. Di Zhao, Y.X. Zhang, HIV-related DNA detection through switching on hybridized quenched fluorescent DNA-Ag nanoclusters, Nanoscale. 10 (2018) 5532–5538.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wu, F. Luo, W. Wen, X. Zhang, S. Wang, Enrichment-Stowage-Cycle Strategy for Ultrasensitive Electrochemiluminescent Detection of HIV-DNA with Wide Dynamic Range, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 12238–12245.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Versatile photoelectrochemical and electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on 3D CdSe QDs-DNA nanonetwork-SnO2 nanoflower coupled with DNA walker amplification for HIV detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Cai, D. Wu, H. Li, G. Jie, H. Zhou, Versatile photoelectrochemical and electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on 3D CdSe QDs-DNA nanonetwork-SnO2 nanoflower coupled with DNA walker amplification for HIV detection, Biosens. Bioelectron. 191 (2021) 113455.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jie Liu is a MS candidate in the college of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. Her main research interests are electrochemiluminescence and biosensors.
Yue Zhang is a Dr. candidate in the college of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. Her main research interests are electrochemiluminescence and biosensors.

Ruo Yuan graduated from Hunan University (China) in 1991. He received his Ph.D. in analytical Chemistry from Hunan University in 1994 for the thesis devoted to chemical sensors. In 1995 he pursued postdoctoral research at the lab of Prof. H. Yoneyama in Osaka University, Japan and in 1998 he worked as a senior research scientist at City University of Hong Kong. In the interim, he devoted to the research on electrochemical sensor, nanomaterial sensor and DNA chip. Since 1999 he has been working as a professor of chemistry in Southwest University, China. His current research interest involves chemical sensor, electrochemical biosensor and electrochemiluminescence biosensor.

Yaqin Chai is a professor of chemistry in Southwest University, China. She received a Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest University, China in 2004. Her main research interests are the development of chemical sensor, electrochemical biosensor and electrochemiluminescence biosensor.
